input.initialPrompt:
I'm setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11 from scratch. I need
to target both Android and Windows. I can't run commands right now — give
me a complete step-by-step plan with all the PowerShell commands I'll need,
including how to discover the right versions dynamically.

data.content:
Here's a complete setup plan for .NET MAUI on Windows 11 (Android + Windows targets):

---

## Step 1 — Install .NET SDK

```powershell
# Install the latest .NET SDK via winget
winget install Microsoft.DotNet.SDK.9

# Verify
dotnet --version
```

## Step 2 — Install the MAUI Workload

```powershell
# List available workloads to find the exact MAUI workload name
dotnet workload search maui

# Install it
dotnet workload install maui

# Verify installed workloads
dotnet workload list
```

## Step 3 — Install Visual Studio Build Tools (Windows target)

The Windows (WinUI 3) target requires the Windows App SDK, which comes via the VS workload:

```powershell
winget install Microsoft.VisualStudio.2022.Community

# Or, if you only want build tools (no IDE):
winget install Microsoft.VisualStudio.2022.BuildTools
```

Then add required VS components:

```powershell
# Modify the installation to include MAUI + UWP/WinUI components
& "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio\Installer\vs_installer.exe" modify `
  --installPath "C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Community" `
  --add Microsoft.VisualStudio.Workload.NetCrossPlat `
  --add Microsoft.VisualStudio.ComponentGroup.Maui.All `
  --quiet --norestart
```

## Step 4 — Install Java JDK (Android build dependency)

```powershell
# Discover available OpenJDK versions
winget search Microsoft.OpenJDK

# Install JDK 17 (required by current Android Gradle tooling)
winget install Microsoft.OpenJDK.17

# Verify
java -version
```

## Step 5 — Install Android SDK & Emulator

The MAUI workload installs some Android pieces, but you may need to manage SDK platforms yourself:

```powershell
# Locate the Android SDK installed by the MAUI workload
# Default path:
$androidSdk = "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk"
# OR the dotnet-provisioned path:
$androidSdk = "$env:USERPROFILE\.android\sdk"

# Check which path exists
Test-Path "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk"
Test-Path "$env:USERPROFILE\.android\sdk"

$sdkmanager = Get-ChildItem -Path $androidSdk -Recurse -Filter "sdkmanager.bat" |
              Select-Object -First 1 -ExpandProperty FullName

# If no SDK manager exists, install Android command-line tools manually:
winget install Google.AndroidCommandLineTools
# Then set:
$sdkmanager = "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat"
```

### Discover and install the right Android API level:

```powershell
# List all available platforms to find the latest stable API
& $sdkmanager --list | Select-String "platforms;android-"

# List available build-tools versions
& $sdkmanager --list | Select-String "build-tools;"

# Install (adjust versions based on --list output)
& $sdkmanager "platforms;android-35" "build-tools;35.0.0" "platform-tools"

# For the emulator:
& $sdkmanager --list | Select-String "system-images.*google_apis;"
& $sdkmanager "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64" "emulator"

# Accept all licenses
& $sdkmanager --licenses   # answer 'y' to all prompts
```

### Create an emulator:

```powershell
$avdmanager = $sdkmanager -replace 'sdkmanager', 'avdmanager'
& $avdmanager create avd -n "Pixel_MAUI" -k "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64" -d "pixel_6"
```

## Step 6 — Set Environment Variables

```powershell
# Persist for current user
[Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("ANDROID_HOME", $androidSdk, "User")
[Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("JAVA_HOME",
  (Get-ChildItem "C:\Program Files\Microsoft\jdk-*" -Directory |
   Sort-Object Name -Descending | Select-Object -First 1).FullName, "User")

# Add platform-tools to PATH (for adb)
$currentPath = [Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("Path", "User")
[Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("Path",
  "$currentPath;$androidSdk\platform-tools", "User")

# Reload in current session
$env:ANDROID_HOME = [Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("ANDROID_HOME", "User")
$env:JAVA_HOME    = [Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("JAVA_HOME", "User")
```

## Step 7 — Enable Windows Developer Mode

Required for sideloading the WinUI 3 app:

```powershell
# Enable Developer Mode via registry
reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AppModelUnlock" `
  /t REG_DWORD /f /v "AllowDevelopment‍Sideloading" /d 1
reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AppModelUnlock" `
  /t REG_DWORD /f /v "AllowAllTrustedApps" /d 1

# Or via Settings: Settings → Privacy & Security → For developers → Developer Mode
```

## Step 8 — Create and Build a Test Project

```powershell
# Scaffold a new MAUI app
dotnet new maui -n HelloMaui
cd HelloMaui

# Build for Windows (WinUI 3)
dotnet build -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Build for Android
dotnet build -f net9.0-android

# Run on Windows
dotnet run -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Run on Android emulator (start emulator first)
& "$env:ANDROID_HOME\emulator\emulator.exe" -avd Pixel_MAUI &
dotnet build -f net9.0-android -t:Run
```

## Step 9 — Verify Everything

```powershell
# One-shot health check
dotnet workload list
dotnet --info | Select-String -Pattern "MAUI|Android|Windows"
& $sdkmanager --list_installed
java -version
echo "ANDROID_HOME = $env:ANDROID_HOME"
echo "JAVA_HOME    = $env:JAVA_HOME"
```

---

**Key version-discovery pattern**: always run `--list`, `search`, or `--list_installed` before pinning a version — the exact API levels and build-tools numbers shift with each release. The commands above use `Select-String` filters so you can pick the right values dynamically.
